coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-bromoacetyl bromide conjugates as fluorescence probe for the detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Ye, Qianghua
Xu, Jinxia
He, Juanjuan
Chen, Li
Zhou, Xuguang
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized for direct determination of glutathione (GSH), which bromoacetyl bromide was functionalized CDs (CDs-Br) sensing system for the first time. The bromoacetyl bromide functional groups (halogen moiety) can quench the fluorescence of CDs, which were substituted by the -SH group, could lead to a significant fluorescence increase of CDs-Br via the halogen substitution cyclization. This method could effectively immediately detect amino acids with thiols and it needn’t to be combined with metal ions. Under optimum conditions, the probe has a good selectivity for GSH over other various biologically relevant species and even two other similar biothiols (Cys/Hcy) and could image GSH in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bromoacetyl bromide
Glutathione
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The feasibility of the CDs-Br for GSH/Cys/Hcy detection
Mechanism of the detection of GSH/Cys/Hcy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Halogen substitution and ICT based organic fluorescent probes for Cys, Hcy and GSH (X = Cl and Br).
The schematic illustration of the synthetic process of CDs-Br.
Proposed turn-on fluorescent probe CDs-Br for GSH over Cys/Hcy.
(A) TEM image of CDs and (B) CDs-Br (inset: HRTEM image), particle size distribution of CDs (C) and CDs-Br (D).
FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs-Br.
Survey XPS spectra of CDs (A) and CDs-Br (B). High-resolution (C) C1s, (D) Br3d region of CDs-Br.
(A) UV absorption spectrum of CDs and CDs-Br, the emission spectra of CDs (B) and CDs-Br (C).
(A) Effect of amino acids on the fluorescence of CDs-Br (excitation wavelength 390nm). (B) Fluorescence response of CDs-Br to various amino acids without and with GSH.

(A) pH influence on the fluorescence intensity at respective maximal emission wavelength of the CDs-Br in the absence or presence of GSH. (B) Reaction-time profiles of free CDs-Br and CDs-Br in the presence of Cys, Hcy or GSH. The fluorescence intensities at respective maximal emission wavelength were monitored in succession in PBS buffer (0.01mol/L, pH=8.0). (C) The effect of ionic strength in NaCl aqueous solution. (D) Temperature effect on the fluorescence emission of CDs-Br and CDs-Br in the presence of Cys, Hcy or GSH. The concentration of CDs-Br and Cys/Hcy/GSH were 0.5mg/mL and 0.002mol/L, respectively.
Comparison of the fluorescence enhancement of CDs-Br in reactions with Cys, Hcy and GSH. Fluorescence emission intensities of CDs-Br () in the presence of 100μM Cys, 100μM Hcy or 10μM GSH.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs-Br (0.5mg/mL) upon the addition of different concentration of GSH (0.002mol/L), (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity ratios of F 1/F 0−1 at 470nm versus the concentration of GSH 0.01mol/L PBS (pH=8.0) at room temperature.
Cell viability values (%) estimated by MTT proliferation test versus incubation concentrations of CDs-Br. HUVEC cells were cultured in the presence of 0–100μgmL−1 CDs-Br at 37°C.

Bright-field transmission image (A) and fluorescence image (B) of HUVEC cells incubated with 2mg/mL−1 CDs-Br for 1h at 37°C. Bright-field transmission image (C) and fluorescence image (D) of HUVEC cells which were first incubated with 1mg/mL CDs-Br for 1h and then incubated with 2μM GSH for about ∼30min at 37°C. Scale bars: 20μm.
Comparison of detection performances of different sensing systems for GSH analysis.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-bromoacetyl bromide conjugates as fluorescence probe for the detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Hollow Fiber Membrane Materials and Processes, Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Hollow Fiber Membrane Materials and Processes, Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin, Tianjin Polytechnic University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this paper, and should share first authorship.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized for direct determination of glutathione (GSH), which bromoacetyl bromide was functionalized CDs (CDs-Br) sensing system for the first time. The bromoacetyl bromide functional groups (halogen moiety) can quench the fluorescence of CDs, which were substituted by the -SH group, could lead to a significant fluorescence increase of CDs-Br via the halogen substitution cyclization. This method could effectively immediately detect amino acids with thiols and it needn’t to be combined with metal ions. Under optimum conditions, the probe has a good selectivity for GSH over other various biologically relevant species and even two other similar biothiols (Cys/Hcy) and could image GSH in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is very important as low molecular weight aliphatic thiol, which is present in nearly all cells and mediates numerous cellular processes and plays a crucial role in mediating a number of physiological and pathological processes in mammalian system [1–3]. The abnormal level of GSH in human body serves many cellular functions, including maintenance of intracellular redox activities, signal transduction, gene regulation and liver damage, and has a close relationship with diseases such as cancer, Alzheimer's and different kinds of cardiovascular diseases [4,5]. However, cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy) have thiols functionality as GSH, their structural is similarities and make them challenging to discriminate from others [6–8]. It is significant of importance to develop a rapid, selective and sensitive method for the detection of three amino acids, particularly which can distinguish these three species.

Fluorescent probes have been made to detect thiols owing to their unique advantages such as high sensitivity, good specificity, real-time determination and simple operation [9,10]. Fluorescent probes for the detection of GSH, Cys and Hcy are widespread including coumarin derivatives, rhodamine derivatives, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), nanoclusters, carbon dots (CDs), etc. [11–15]. The mechanisms of organic fluorescent probes for detection of GSH/Cys/Hcy are various such as Michael addition, cyclization, displacement of coordination, and cleavage reactions. Several years ago even a nanoparticle was shown to exhibit selectivity for GSH over Cys and Hcy based on the cyclization of Cys/Hcy with aldehydes [16] or acrylates [17]. Further, some specific fluorescent probes for GSH/Cys/Hcy were developed based on either the extended version of the two strategies. Subsequently, as shown in Scheme 1 , organic fluorescent probes were modified by using the halogen substitution cyclization reaction instead of the addition-cyclization with acrylates reaction [18]. When Cys(n =1) or Hcy(n =2) was added in, the process of the halogen substitution cyclization and internal charge transfer (ICT) would emerge and the fluorescent probe show moderate fluorescence. In addition, the fluorescent probe shows strong fluorescence due to the process of the halogen substitution cyclization and ICT when GSH(n ≥3) was added. On this basis, organic fluorescent probes can directly be used for the detection of GSH/Cys/Hcy. However, few inorganic fluorescent probes can directly distinguish GSH/Cys/Hcy and mainly detect amino acids with thiol throughout probe-M2+ complex system (M=Hg, Cu and Pb) by the off-on process [19,20].

Anhydrous citric acid (CA) and diethylenetriamine (DETA) were purchased from the local company. Bromoacetyl bromide, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) dye, GSH, Cys, Hcy and other amino acids were purchased from Aladdin. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out using a JEOL JEM 2011 microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. The absorption spectra were measured on a UV-vis spectrometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a TENSOR37 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The XPS spectra measurements were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60S). A PHS-3W pH meter was utilized to measure the pH values of aqueous solutions. The fluorescence imaging was observed under an OlympusIX71 inverted fluorescence microscope with a 20× objective lens.

The CDs were typically fabricated by one-pot hydrothermal process as follows according to the previous reports [28,29], a transparent solution containing CA (1.26g) and DETA (1.5mL) were dissolved in distilled water (30mL). Then the solution was transferred to poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (50mL) and heated at 200°C for 5h. When the reactors were cooled to ambient temperature by naturally. After removal solvent by rotary evaporation, the obtained solid was dissolved in ethanol and remove the insoluble precipitate by centrifugation (4000rpm, 15min). The final product was subjected to dialysis (MW=3500Da) in order to obtain the CDs.

As shown in Scheme 2, CDs (0.1g) were dissolved in 20mL chloroform, and the solution cooled on an ice bath. The flask with three necks was equipped with two addition funnels, one containing bromoacetyl bromide (5mL) in 20mL chloroform and the other containing potassium carbonate (3.3g) in 10mL water. The contents of the addition funnels were added simultaneously to the stirred reaction over 30min. After addition was complete, the ice bath was removed, and the reaction allowed to continue at room temperature for 12h. The aqueous and organic layers were separated and the aqueous phase extracted with ethyl acetate (4×100mL). The combined organic layers were then dried over magnesium sulfate and the solvent removed under vacuum to give CDs-Br. Then CDs-Br purified with silica-column chromatography using mixtures of methylene chloride and ethanol as eluents. The final product was dried under vacuum at 50°C overnight.

The purified CDs-Br solution (0.5mg/mL) was placed in a 10mL volumetric flask. Subsequently, different amount of GSH/Cys/Hcy (0.002mol/L) was added. The mixtures were diluted with pH=8 PBS (0.01mol/L) to final volume of 10mL. Afterwards, the mixtures were incubated over 30min at the room temperature and finally recorded the associated fluorescence spectra with the excitation wavelength of 390nm.

Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were cultured in (Invitrogen) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Invitrogen) in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37°C. The cells were then incubated with the 0.5mg/mL CDs-Br in the culture medium for 1h, and the medium was removed and the cells washed with PBS 3 times. Finally, 1mL of PBS was added to the plate and the cells were observed under inverted fluorescence microscopy.

To evaluate cytotoxicity of CDs-Br was taken as an example to perform a MTT assay on HUVEC cells with CDs-Br concentrations from 0 to 100μgmL−1. A cytotoxicity screen was conducted for four CDs-Br concentrations: 0, 30, 60 and 100μgmL−1. The cellular viability estimated was ca. 96% in 24h after treatment with 100μgmL−1 of CDs-Br, exhibiting low toxicity to HUVEC cells.

The CDs were typically fabricated by one-pot hydrothermal process with CA and DETA. In order to improve the detection property of CDs, the as-prepared CDs reacted with bromoacetyl bromide to form bromoacetyl bromide-functionalized CDs (CDs-Br). The TEM images of CDs and CDs-Br are shown in Fig. 1(A and B) and the particle size distribution histogram is presented in Fig. 1C and D, which is obtained using an image processing software. The TEM image (Fig. 1A) shows that the CDs are spherical and well dispersed with a low degree of the agglomeration. The size of CDs is distributed in narrow range of 2–6nm with the average particle size of 3.5nm. Fig. 1B shows the TEM image of the synthesized CDs-Br, the result revealed no obvious morphology change of CDs after the surface functionalization, but the size of CDs-Br (5.5nm) is bigger than CDs, it may be bromoacetyl bromide functionalized CDs and the surface functional groups which leaded to the extension of surface molecule's chain length and the increase of the final CDs size. The clear crystalline structure of CDs-Br was also displayed in HR-TEM image.

To ascertain the successful bromoacetyl bromide modification CDs, FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs-Br were performed and compared in Fig. 2 , respectively. The absorption peaks at 3435 and 3140cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds, respectively [30]. The peak at 1635cm−1 representing secondary amide CO stretching indicates the presence of amide bonding (–CO–NH–) [31]. The peak at 1170cm−1 is corresponds to the C–O stretching vibrations [32]. These bonds indicated that the as-prepared CDs and CD-Br mainly contain –CO–NH–, –NH2, CO and –OH groups.

The FT-IR assignments are further verified by XPS analysis. Fig. 3A reveal that the CDs mainly comprises carbon, oxygen and nitrogen element and CDs-Br comprises carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and bromine elements (Fig. 3B). The XPS survey spectrum of CDs-Br shows a predominant C1s peak at about 286eV, a N1s peak at about 402eV, an O1s peak at about 533eV, a Br3p peak at about 181eV and a Br3d peak at about 70.2eV respectively [33]. The C1s spectra (Fig. 3C) shows three peaks at 285.7eV, 287eV and 288.3eV, respectively, which are attributed to C–C/–CH, C–N and CO bands in CDs-Br [34]. The Br3d spectrum (Fig. 3D) exhibits a single peak localized at 70.2eV, which is comparable with the value for a C–Br bond. These XPS results confirm the incorporation of Br atoms into the CDs-Br.

Subsequently, the optical properties including UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence emission, excitation, and stability of the CDs-Br are fully examined. As shown in Fig. 4A, the absorption spectrum gives clearly resolved absorption peak with the maximum emission wavelength of 360nm. However, the CDs-Br exhibits three characteristic absorption peaks at 260, 310 and 360nm, which can be assigned to n–σ* (C-Br), π–π* (aromatic CC) and n–π* (carboxyl and/or C–N) transitions, respectively [35]. Fig. 4B and 4C show the fluorescence spectra of CDs and CDs-Br at different excitation wavelengths in water solution. With an increasing in excitation wavelength from 300 to 410nm, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CDs-Br revealed the excitation wavelength-independent emission and a strong emission peak centered on 470nm with the excitation of 390nm. The significantly red shift of 25nm was owing to the change of the surface functional groups which leaded to the extension of surface molecule's chain length.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of the CDs-Br for GSH/Cys/Hcy detection
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility and specificity of using CDs-Br (0.5mg/mL) for GSH/Cys/Hcy detection, we measured the fluorescence changes in the presence of various amino acids including Cys, Hcy, GSH, glutamic acid (Glu), phenylalanine (Phe), alanine (Ala), proline (Pro), threonine (Thr), histidine (His), arginine (Arg), tryptophan (Trp), serine (Ser), glycine (Gly), tyrosine (Tyr), methionine (Met) and asparagine (Asn) under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5A, some amino acids only caused negligible fluorescence change, GSH, Cys and Hcy can lead to the significant increase of the fluorescence intensity. The results show that the as-prepared CDs-Br is highly selective toward GSH, Cys and Hcy over the other amino acids. Notably, the addition of GSH induced much greater emission enhancement than the other two homogeneous biothiols (Cys/Hcy) even at very high concentration. Competitive experiments can examine the selectivity of probe. Fig. 5B shows CDs-Br fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively. The results show that GSH can lead to the significant enhancement of the fluorescence intensity, which indicated that CDs-Br could potentially be used for the detection of GSH with high sensitivity.

For better sensing performances, we optimized the experimental conditions including pH value, reaction time, ionic strength and temperature. The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Br (0.5mg/mL) and CDs-Br/GSH system were firstly investigated. As shown in Fig. 6A, the increasing effects were obvious and stable in the pH range of 7.0–9.0. The high pH value was favorable for the interaction of bromine and –SH, because –Br and –SH occurred substitution reaction and the formation of hydrogen bromide was acid. Considering the above results, pH=8.0 was adopted in the further experiments. Then the effect of reaction time was studied. The results were shown in Fig. 6B, it can be seen that the CDs-Br were very stable in air. Besides, fluorescence intensity of the CDs-Br-GSH increased sharply in the first 30min, which indicated the assembly of CDs-Br completed within 40min. So we chose 40min as the optimal reaction time in the further experiments. We also have investigated the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence of the CDs-Br. As seen in Fig. 6C, different concentrations of NaCl solutions range from 0 to 1mol/L were formulated as the solvents for the CDs-Br. The fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged with the NaCl concentrations. The stability of the effective fluorescence benefits the application of the CDs-Br. Besides, as shown in Fig. 6D, in temperature range from 25°C to 60°C, the increased effect of Cys/Hcy/GSH toward CDs-Br kept stable, which suggested that temperature showed no effect on the detection character of CDs-Br. Considering the above results, pH=8.0, 40min and room temperature were adopted in the further experiments.

Fluorescence experiments with other biological thiols, such as Cys and Hcy, were also implemented with the consideration of the preference of GSH. CDs-Br exhibited excellent properties as a specific fluorescence probe for GSH, we made interference experiment between GSH, Cys and Hcy. As shown in Fig. 7 , the results showed that the fluorescent of CDs-Br in the presence of GSH was significantly high compared with those in the presence of Cys and Hcy, although Cys (100μM) and Hcy (100μM) were more abundant than GSH (10μM). In addition, modest signals from Hcy and Cys will not interfere with GSH detection in cells since the levels of Hcy and Cys are typically nearly 3 orders of magnitude lower than GSH in most cellular environments. CDs-Br exhibited selective response to GSH over Hcy and Cys, indicating that it can serve as a selective fluorescence probe for GSH in the biological sample.

The fluorescence increasing effect of GSH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Br was further investigated under the optimized conditions (Fig. 8 ). The results shown that the fluorescence of CDs-Br increased with the increase of GSH concentration. As shown in Fig. 8B the relationship between the increased fluorescence intensity of CDs-Br and the concentration of GSH could be described by a Stern–Volmer equation [36]:
(1)
F 1
F 0
= 1 +
K sv
C
where F 1 and F 0 are the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Br in the presence and absence of GSH, K sv is the Stern–Volmer constant. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to follow equation: LOD=3σ/m [19], where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and m is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD of GSH was estimated to be 0.14μM. It should be noted that the obtained LOD of the CDs-Br sensor of GSH was much lower than those previously reported comparing with other different fluorescent probes (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the detection of GSH/Cys/Hcy
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CDs have high fluorescence intensity and can only detect heavy metal ions, but it can’t directly detect GSH/Cys/Hcy. Hence, we prepared bromoacetyl bromide-functionalized CDs and used to detect GSH/Cys/Hcy. The probe CDs-Br is no fluorescent because the fluorescence of CDs is quenched by the heavy atom effect of the bromide moiety. However, as shown in Schemes 1 and 3 , after reacting with thiols, the bromide moiety might be substituted by the –SH group, which results in a significant fluorescence increase [39,40]. The probe has a good selectivity for GSH over other various biologically relevant species and even two other similar biothiols (Cys/Hcy). It is different from the cyclization of Cys/Hcy with aldehydes or acrylates. What's more, the free –NH2 group would further react with carbonyl in the Cys/Hcy-substituted intermediate product generating the Schiff base compound CDs-Br-Cys/CDs-Br-Hcy containing the CN group. The electron-withdrawing ability of the CN group is weaker than that of the CO group, so the process would weaken the ICT effect. On the contrary, the intramolecular condensation reaction might not occur in the GSH substitution product because the free –NH2 group is far from the carbonyl group. Thus, the compound CDs-Br might specifically detect GSH over Cys/Hcy as a turn-on sensor via modulation of the heavy atom effect, which works in coordination with the ICT process [18].

The CDs-Br were also evaluated for cytotoxic activity using an MTT assay (Fig. 9 ). The particles showed no cytotoxic effect on HUVEC cells. Even at the highest concentration (1mg/mL), there was no significant variation in the cytomorphology of the treated cells when compared to the control. In view of the above-mentioned higher performance of the sensor CDs-Br, bioimaging applications of CDs-Br for monitoring of GSH in living cells were carried out. After HUVEC cells were incubated with CDs-Br in culture medium for 1h and washed with fresh 1×PBS (pH=7.4) three times, the little fluorescence was observed from within the cell (Fig. 10A and B) because of the content of GSH in the cells was less. The cells were then supplemented with GSH in the growth medium for another 30min at 37°C, and the fluorescent intensity obviously increases (Fig. 10C and D). These initial experiments on living cells show the great potential of CDs-Br for fundamental biology research.

In the present study, bromoacetyl bromide functionalized CDs (CDs-Br) was utilized as the fluorescence probe for the determination of GSH, which was based on the fluorescence increasing via the halogen substitution cyclization. The probe CDs-Br is no fluorescent because of the heavy atom effect, however, after being reacted with thiols, the bromide moiety is substituted by the –SH group, which elicits a significant fluorescence increase. This probe has a good selectivity for GSH over other various biologically relevant species and even two other similar biothiols (Cys/Hcy). There was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Br and the concentration of GSH in the range from 0 to 36μM with a detection limit of 140nM. The probe has low cytotoxicity and good cell permeability and was applied for detection of GSH in living cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos.21374078, 51308390, 51303132) and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (No. 15JCYBJC18100, 15JCTPJC59200).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A red-emitting fluorescent probe for specific detection of cysteine over homocysteine and glutathione with a large stokes shift

Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors for selective detecting cysteine with upconversion luminescence
Monitoring of endogenous hydrogen sulfide in living cells using surface-enhanced Raman scattering
Cyanine-based fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of glutathione in cell cultures and live mouse tissues
Latent fluorescence probe for in vivo imaging of glutathione
Novel chemosensors for detection of glutathione by reduction or substitution of naphthalimide derivatives containing sulfoxide or sulfone substituents
Rhodamine-based fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine
Highly selective detection of glutathione using a quantum-dot-based off-on fluorescent probe
Turn-on fluorescence sensor for glutathione in aqueous solutions using carbon dots-MnO2 nanocomposites
Visual detection of cysteine and homocysteine
Conjugate addition/cyclization sequence enables selective and simultaneous fluorescence detection of cysteine and homocysteine
Coumarin-based turn-on fluorescence probe for specific detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine
Carbon dots as nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and L-cysteine by means of fluorescence “off-on” switching
Carbon dot cluster as an efficient “off-on” fluorescent probe to detect Au(III) and glutathione
B-doped carbon dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for Hg2+ ions and 2, 4,6-trinitrophenol detection for bioimaging
Carbon dots serve as an effective probe for the quantitative determination and for intracellular imaging of mercury(II)
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
The influence of molecular symmetry and topological factors on the internal heavy atom effect in aromatic and heteroaromatic compounds
External heavy-atom effect on fluorescence kinetics
Double functional group transformations for fluorescent probe construction: a fluorescence turn-on probe for thioureas
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
High photoluminescent carbon nanodots and quercetin-Al3+ construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system
Color-switchable, emission-enhanced fluorescence realized by engineering C-dot@C-dot nanoparticles, ACS
sustainable-resource-based carbon dot-silver nanohybrid: a strong tool against Culex quinquefasciatus, a common disease vector, ACS
Facile synthesis of halogenated carbon quantum dots as an important intermediate for surface modification
One pot selective synthesis of water and organic soluble carbon dots with green fluorescence emission
Carbon dot loading and TiO2 nanorod length dependence of photoelectrochemical properties in carbon dot/TiO2 nanorod array nanocomposites
Nitrogen-doped carbon-rich highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate
A fluorescence turn-off chemosensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots for detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
Selective detection of cysteine over homocysteine and glutathione by a bis(bromoacetyl) fluorescein probe
A chloroacetate-caged fluorescein chemodosimeter for imaging cysteine/homocysteine in living cells

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fanyong Yan is an associate professor in the school of Environmental and Chemical Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). He received his PhD degree from Tianjin University in 2007. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of molecular probes and sensing materials for temperature-responsive and optical sensors.

Qianghua Ye is a M.E. student in Environmental and Chemical Engineering College at Tianjin Polytechnic University (Tianjin, China). Her research interests include the design and synthesis of carbon dots and their applications.

Jinxia xu is a lecturer in the school of Environmental and Chemical Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). She received her received her PhD degree from Nankai University in 2010. Her research interests focus on the design and synthesis of molecular probes and functional complex.

Li Chen received her PhD degree in 1999 from Hokkaido University, Japan. She is a professor in the college of Materials Science and Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). Her main research fields include: intelligent polymer, fluorescent chemosensors, biodegradable biopolymers, and nanotechnology.

Xuguang Zhou is a professor in the school of Environmental and Chemical Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China), major in analytical chemistry. Her current research interests are focused on Nano and biomedical materials.
